title: When You Feel Tired or Lost, How to Care for Yourself
content:
Many times, when we feel confused, lost, or exhausted, we assume we are not doing well enough.
In reality, it is often our body sending a signal: you need to take care of yourself. Feeling tired or lost is not a sign of failure. It is a reminder to slow down and care for yourself.

There are many ways to care for ourselves. We can receive support from parents, partners, or friends. However, everyone has their own limitations. Sometimes they may not notice what we need, or the support they offer may not match what we actually need right now. That’s why the most important skill is learning to care for yourself. When you develop your own ways of caring, you become more steady in holding yourself through difficult moments.

First, accept this truth: You are worthy of care.
Not because you are perfect or accomplished, but simply because you exist. You do not need to earn it.

So how can you practice this? When you feel tired, allow yourself to rest. When you feel hungry, eat properly. These are the most basic actions. When you need affirmation and support, try the following approaches:

Understand how self-affirmation works
Research shows that when we consciously affirm ourselves, the brain activates areas related to self-worth and reward. This process reduces the impact of self-criticism and helps us recover from stress more quickly. In simple terms, self-affirmation is not self-deception — it is a way of telling your brain: “I have value, and I deserve to be treated kindly.”

Download the Medium App
Recall people who have supported you unconditionally
Many people think of a grandparent or someone who once told them, “You are good just as you are.” Bringing that memory to mind can help restore a sense of strength.

Write down small moments of warmth
Notice and record small things — a stranger’s smile, someone holding the door for you, or a kind message. These moments remind you that care exists around you.

Practice mindfulness meditation
Sit quietly and focus on your breath. When thoughts arise, observe them without judgment and gently return your attention to your breathing. Even five to ten minutes a day can help you see yourself more clearly and reduce self-criticism.

Do a self-affirmation exercise
Consciously seeing and affirming yourself can help you feel stronger. Regular practice builds a more stable sense of inner support. You don’t need to do all of these. Choose the methods that feel right for you at the moment. The key is to take action.

When you start caring for yourself and feel supported, you will gradually have more strength to care for others as well.
Caring for yourself is not selfish — it is how energy flows.

So when you feel tired or lost,
allow yourself to slow down
and take action to care for yourself. You deserve to be cared for. Remember, the world and I care about you.
